text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes tissue flakes and fluid in the right subhepatic and subdiaphragmatic areas. After suctioning the fluid, the exploration reveals more tissue flakes. The surgeon traces the antrum of the stomach and identifies a small perforation on the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, with gastric contents visibly leaking from the site.

The surgeon identifies the area of perforation in the duodenum, characterized by visible red mucosa. Surrounding tissue flakes are cleared, and the margins of the perforation are trimmed. Tissue near the perforated area is collected for biopsy and histopathological examination.

The surgeon clears the surrounding tissue flakes from the perforation site and uses 2-0 PDS sutures to place three stay sutures: one on the proximal margin, one through the middle, and one on the lower part of the perforation. Each suture is taken with a deep, through-and-through bite to ensure secure closure of the defect.

The surgeon uses an instrument from the right anterior axillary port for liver retraction, providing exposure to take full-thickness bites through the defect. Three sutures are placed over the defect area. The surgeon selects a vascularized and mobile omentum to cover the defect, asking the assistant to hold its tip in place while securing the first suture knot. After securing the first knot, the omentum remains in position, allowing the surgeon to proceed with additional knots without instrument crowding.

The surgeon secures the knot and divides the excess suture, then takes a second bite. The distal suture is tightened to prevent loosening, while the second suture holds the omentum in place. All knots are positioned to ensure the omentum remains securely over the defect.

The surgeon ensures optimal pressure is applied to the omentum while tightening the sutures to maintain its blood supply. After securing all sutures, a thorough peritoneal lavage is performed under laparoscopic vision.

The surgeon performs peritoneal lavage using warm normal saline, ensuring thorough cleaning of the peritoneal cavity. The procedure is conducted with a magnified laparoscopic view, utilizing HD monitors for visualization. The surgeon and camera assistant adjust their positions to effectively lavage different areas, including the upper abdominal cavity, pelvis, and paracolic gutters. The lavage continues until the returning fluid is clear, indicating adequate cleaning.

The surgeon encounters dilated small bowel loops due to perforated peritonitis, which may complicate bowel retraction and lavage. Careful manipulation is performed using atraumatic instruments to avoid damage while retracting the bowel.

The surgeon places two drains after completing the lavage: one drain is positioned in the hepatorenal pouch in the sub-hepatic area through the right anterior axillary port, and another 24 French drain is inserted into the pelvis through the left pararectal five-millimeter port.
